name: Public Construction
description: Assessment of involvement in contracts for government construction / infrastructure in sensitive countries (SCAP jurisdictions): public buildings, transport infrastructure, and public-services infrastructure. Qualification via direct engagement (25% of wealth / business revenues / income, whichever is higher) or substantial connection (ownership of 25% or more, or senior executive / executive board role in a SIAP relevant business).
field: public construction, public infrastructure, government construction projects, public works, infrastructure development, government-funded construction, public sector construction contracts
comment: Remediated per wiki/public-construction-gap-analysis (D1-D8): sensitive-country prompt replaces the scap_check compute placeholder; conservative rule encoded (government level not determinable -> in scope); substantial connection anchored to a SIAP relevant business; SC3 MI -> missing_information; SC1 narrowed to senior exec / executive board with is-or-was tense; DE4 gained the business-revenues limb; explicit exclusion chain added; typos and scope wording fixed. Threshold phrased '25% or more' per policy 2.1 definition — note the policy itself is internally inconsistent ('more than 25%' in 2.2/2.3); flagged to policy owners. Cooldown block retained: this policy defines cooldown paths (a) and (b).

involvement.text: Is or was the client, through his activity, linked, now or in the past, to contracts for government construction or infrastructure, including the purchase of goods, services, or works by a government agency? Answer Yes if any such link — current or historical — is described, No if the activity is clearly unrelated to government construction or infrastructure, and Missing Information if the description is too unclear to decide. Client notes: {activity}
involvement.summary: Public-construction involvement - is the client linked to government construction / infrastructure contracts?

prompts: Does the client's business consist of post-construction services for COMPLETED buildings and infrastructure — ongoing maintenance, facility management (operating finished buildings), janitorial services, or inspection services? Answer Yes only if such after-completion services are what the notes describe as the client's actual activity. Answer No in all other cases — including construction project managers, site supervisors and anyone who manages or coordinates building works, and anyone who is or was a construction contractor or owner of one. Client notes: {activity}

Does the client's business consist of building privately owned and privately financed infrastructure (e.g. private stadiums, shopping malls) with no government-funded work? Answer Yes only if that is what the notes describe as the client's actual activity. Answer No in all other cases, including when the client — now or in the past — also carried out government-funded construction or infrastructure work, even if that involvement has since ended. Client notes: {activity}

Does the client operate infrastructure under a concession agreement financed with the operator's OWN capital (recovered through user fees such as tolls), where the construction work is NOT paid with public funds, subsidies or a public-private partnership? Answer Yes only if such a self-financed concession is what the notes describe. Answer No in all other cases, including concessions paid from public funds and when the client — now or in the past — carried out government construction work paid with public funds, even if that involvement has since ended. Client notes: {activity}

Is the client's own business the selling or delivery of building materials, goods or equipment (e.g. cement, steel, machinery) to construction companies — a trading or supply business rather than one that carries out construction works? Answer Yes only if such a supply/trading business is what the notes describe. Answer No in all other cases, including when the client is or was a contractor, subcontractor, owner or manager of a construction business. Client notes: {activity}

Has the client NEVER had any construction activity, role, stake or business of their own — neither now nor in the past — with their only connection to construction being money received from a relative or other third party involved in it (for example family support payments)? Answer Yes only for such never-involved recipients. Answer No whenever the client works or worked in construction, manages or managed building projects, owns or owned a stake in a construction business, or receives fees for construction work. Client notes: {activity}
exclusion_check.comment: Policy 2.3/2.4 exclusions made explicit: post-construction contracts, private infrastructure, own-capital concessions (footnote 3: a concession company doing construction paid with public funds stays IN scope), suppliers, pure fund recipients. Runs before the country checks so a definite exclusion is never masked by an unknown country nexus.

government_level_check.text: Do or did the client's government construction / infrastructure contracts — current or past — involve a federal, central or national government entity (the highest level of government)? Answer No only if the notes clearly state that the contracts are or were solely with regional or local government levels. Answer Yes if federal / central / national government involvement is stated. Answer Missing Information if the level of government cannot be established from the notes. Client notes: {activity}
government_level_check.summary: Government-level check - only federal/central/national contracts are in scope; if the level cannot be established, the conservative approach applies (in scope)
government_level_check.comment: Policy 2.3 conservative rule: 'In case we are not able to establish the level of government contracting our client, a conservative approach should be applied, and we should consider the client as in scope' — therefore Missing Information CONTINUES toward qualification instead of exiting (the pre-remediation binary prompt inverted this).

sensitive_country_check.text: Is or was the government awarding the client's current or past construction / infrastructure contracts that of a sensitive country — a country listed as sensitive for money laundering purposes on the Financial Crime Country Risk Information Table (SCAP jurisdiction, i.e. a country with heightened corruption / money-laundering risk and weaker public-procurement oversight)? Consider the stated countries of the projects and contracting governments. Answer Missing Information if the countries involved are not stated in the client notes — do not guess Yes or No. Client notes: {activity}
sensitive_country_check.summary: Sensitive-country check - the category only applies to government construction in SCAP jurisdictions
sensitive_country_check.comment: Replaces the former scap_check compute placeholder (gap D1): the category-defining criterion is now actually asked. If a structured country-table compute exists in production, run it first and keep this prompt as the documented fallback.

prompts: Is or was the client involved through his activity: {activity} in the construction of public buildings such as administrative offices, national court buildings and military bases ?
Is or was the client involved through his activity: {activity} in the construction of public transport infrastructure (e.g. roads, railways, bridges, tunnels, airports) ?
Is or was the client involved through his activity: {activity} in the construction of public-services infrastructure (e.g. water supply and treatment, sewage treatment, electrical grids, dams) ?
verification.comment: Typos fixed ('construciton'); limb 3 reworded from 'providing public services' to 'construction of public-services infrastructure' — operating a utility is not in scope, building one is (gap D8).

business_share_check.text: What share of the revenues, turnover or receipts of the business described comes or came from government construction / infrastructure projects? Assess the business as it was during the client's current or former involvement. Count receipts invoiced to main contractors when working as a subcontractor on such projects, and payments from public funds or the state budget under a concession. If a percentage or share for these government projects is stated, use it: answer Yes if it is 25% or more, and No if it is below 25%. If no figure is given but the notes describe these government projects as the business's main, principal or majority activity (or the government as its principal client), answer Yes. Do NOT use the share of the client's personal wealth linked to construction — that is a different measure than the business's revenue share. Answer Missing Information when neither a figure nor such a statement is present. Client notes: {activity}
business_share_check.summary: SIAP relevant business gate - share of the business from sensitive-country government construction
business_share_check.comment: Gap D3 anchor. Single prompt in the SC3-proven 'if stated, use it' skeleton — both the compound prompt and the 3-limb chain oscillated under llama-class models. MI falls through to the direct-engagement chain, tagged unclear_business_relevance so the D3 uncertainty (SIAP-relevant-business status never confirmed) stays visible in generated explanations even though routing continues.

substantial_connection_1.text: Is or was the client holding a position in the SENIOR executive management (e.g. CEO, CFO, COO, CTO, CSO, Managing Director, heads of divisions) or acting as an EXECUTIVE director on the board of directors of the SIAP relevant business described in his activity: {activity}? Honorary, non-executive, supervisory-only or purely advisory roles do NOT count — answer No for those. Answer Missing Information if the seniority or executive nature of the role is unclear.
substantial_connection_1.comment: Gap D5: narrowed from 'any executive power' to the policy footnote definition (senior executive management / executive board director); 'is or was' so former executives reach the cooldown branch.

substantial_connection_2.text: Is or was the client an owner, co-owner or shareholder (of any size, including a minority stake) of the company or business described in his activity: {activity}? Answer Yes if any ownership stake or shareholding is mentioned, No if the client clearly holds no stake, and Missing Information if it is unclear.

substantial_connection_3.text: What is the client's ownership share (percentage) in the business described in his activity? If a percentage is stated in the notes, use it: answer Yes if it is 25% or more, and No if it is below 25%. Only when NO percentage is stated but the client is described as THE owner of the business, treat it as 100% and answer Yes. Answer Missing Information when neither a percentage nor sole ownership is stated. Client notes: {activity}
substantial_connection_3.comment: Gap D4: Missing Information now routes to missing_information — an unknown ownership percentage must surface, not drift through the DE chain into an exit (same fix as art-dealing PR1/C3). Threshold '25% or more' per policy 2.1; the 2.2/2.3 'more than 25%' inconsistency is flagged to policy owners.

prompts: Does (or did) the client earn 25% or more of his total yearly personal income from the government construction / infrastructure activity described — counting salary, fees, profits or dividends from it? If a share of his income from this activity is stated, use it: answer Yes if it is 25% or more, and No if it is below 25%. Answer Missing Information only when no income share is stated in the notes. Client context: {activity}

Does (or did) the government construction / infrastructure activity described account for 25% or more of the client's total personal wealth — counting stakes in and assets of such businesses? If a share of his wealth linked to this activity is stated, use it: answer Yes if it is 25% or more, and No if it is below 25%. Answer Missing Information only when no wealth share is stated in the notes. Client context: {activity}

Do (or did) 25% or more of the total revenues/turnover of the client's own business come from government construction / infrastructure projects — including payments from public funds, state subsidies or budget milestones under a concession? If the client IS a company, this means the company's own revenues. Answer No if the client does not own such a business, or if the business's revenues clearly come from other lines of activity. Answer Missing Information only when the client has such a business but its revenue split is not stated. Client context: {activity}
direct_engagement_4.comment: Gap D6: business-revenues limb added (policy triad: wealth / business revenues / income, whichever is higher); prompts name the SIAP activity explicitly and carry MI instructions; threshold '25% or more'.

final_status_check.text: Based on the activity: {activity}, is the client's public-construction SIAP involvement past-only? Answer Yes only where the available information explicitly indicates that the qualifying executive, board, ownership, control, or operational involvement ended in the past. Answer No where the available information explicitly indicates current qualifying public-construction involvement through ongoing executive, board, management, operational, control, or ownership involvement, including ownership of 25% or more, controlling ownership, or active staged disposal of a public-construction business while retaining control of that business. Passive ownership below 25%, residual minority shareholdings, earn-out rights, rights to future payments, contractual rights to sell shares, historical proceeds, and other passive economic interests do not by themselves constitute current qualifying involvement. Passive ownership below 25%, historical proceeds, earn-out rights, rights to future payments, and historical source of wealth do not by themselves constitute current qualifying involvement. Answer Missing Information only where the available information does not allow a determination as to whether the qualifying involvement is current or has ended.

cooled_down_check.text: Based on the activity: {activity}, does the client satisfy the public-construction cooldown rule? Answer Yes only where the available information reasonably supports at least one of the following: (1) the qualifying SIAP involvement ended at least 24 months ago and there is no evidence of current qualifying public-construction involvement; — condition (1) applies IRRESPECTIVE of how much of the client's wealth still stems from the past involvement — or (2) the qualifying SIAP involvement ended at least 12 months ago, public-construction-linked wealth has been below 25% for at least 12 months, and there is no evidence of current qualifying public-construction involvement. Historical source of wealth, earn-out rights, contractual rights to sell, and passive ownership below 25% do not prevent cooldown. Answer No where the available information reasonably indicates that the cooldown requirements are not satisfied or that current qualifying public-construction involvement exists. Answer Missing Information whenever the timing of cessation of the qualifying involvement cannot be reasonably determined from the available information and cooldown eligibility therefore cannot be assessed. Do not assume that cooldown requirements are satisfied solely because the involvement appears historical.
cooled_down_check.comment: One clarifying clause added to the dev template: condition (1) applies irrespective of residual wealth share (small models conflated it with condition (2)'s wealth test). Flag to dev for standardization.
